channel.title: About Me on Edward Isayev
channel.description: Recent content in About Me on Edward Isayev

item.title: Notes
item.description:
Here you can find some expository notes on areas related to my thesis, which will eventually include toric geometry, orbifold cohomology, log geometry and more. They are deliberately informal and highly example-driven (frequently weighted projective space), written to complement the standard references. Any mistakes made herein are entirely my own, and corrections would be greatly appreciated to my email.

Equivariant Orbifold Cohomology (Forthcoming)
#

Notes on the practical ways of computing torus-equivariant Chen–Ruan cohomology for toric stacks, such as weighted projective space in the sense of Coates–Iritani–Jiang. In particular, they explore the combinatorial description of certain toric Deligne–Mumford stacks, and using this data, compute the full torus-equivariant cohomology ring. These concepts are worked out in full for \(\mathbb{P}(1,2,2)\).

item.title: Research
item.description:
My research is centered on the Gromov–Witten theory of Calabi–Yau hypersurfaces, especially the structural predictions that arise from the BCOV conjectures.

In particular, I work with singular hypersurfaces in weighted projective space, and aim to establish their higher-genus structure by purely algebro-geometric means. I am especially interested in Yamaguchi–Yau polynomiality, the holomorphic anomaly equation and orbifold regularity.

For a gentle introduction to this area, the Expository Notes section of my website offers various short notes on the relevant prerequisites, such as orbifold cohomology and the \(I\)-function.

item.title: Talks
item.description:
A list of seminar and reading-group talks I’ve given. The accompanying handwritten lecture notes are available upon request.

Counting is Hard: A Gentle Introduction to Enumerative Geometry
#

GIST Seminar · Spring 2026

Enumerative geometry is a beautiful area of mathematics, relating ideas from algebraic geometry, mathematical physics, symplectic topology and much more. In this talk, I will give a gentle introduction to the topic from the point of view of algebraic geometry, showcasing some of the key players involved, and explaining where the main difficulties arise. Despite being called “enumerative” geometry, very few numbers will appear, and I hope to explain why that’s okay.
